turns.content: I attended a concert last evening that I am still thinking about this morning. I wanted to share the experience while it is fresh.
I'd love to hear about it. What was the concert?

The Minnesota Orchestra performed Beethoven's Seventh Symphony. I have listened to recordings of the Seventh more times than I can count, but hearing it performed live in Orchestra Hall is an entirely different encounter. The acoustics in that space do something to the sound that no recording can capture. You feel the lower registers in your chest.

Orchestra Hall does have remarkable acoustics. And the Seventh is one of those pieces that really benefits from the live experience — there's so much rhythmic energy that you almost need to feel physically. Which movement stayed with you the most?

The second movement, the Allegretto. It is often described as mournful, but I have never heard it that way. To me it is something closer to dignity in the face of sorrow. The theme builds through those variations with such patience. Each repetition adds a layer without ever abandoning the restraint of the original statement. It is, if you will forgive the analogy, surgical in its precision.

That's a fitting analogy, actually. The Allegretto has that quality of absolute deliberateness — every note is placed with purpose, and the emotional impact comes from the accumulation rather than any single dramatic gesture. Wagner called the Seventh the "apotheosis of the dance," but the second movement is where Beethoven shows that dance can also be solemn and processional. Did the orchestra's interpretation bring out anything you hadn't noticed before?

The conductor — I believe it was Osmo Vanska, though I should confirm — took the Allegretto slower than I am accustomed to. At first I was skeptical. But the slower tempo allowed the inner voices to emerge more clearly. The viola and cello lines underneath the main theme were given room to breathe, and I heard harmonic relationships I had missed in faster performances. It reminded me that what you hear depends on the space you give it.

That's a wonderful observation. A slower tempo in the Allegretto is a risk — it can tip into heaviness if the players don't maintain the forward motion within each phrase. But when it works, exactly as you describe, it opens up the texture and lets you hear the piece as a conversation between voices rather than a single melody with accompaniment. "What you hear depends on the space you give it" is a beautiful principle that applies well beyond music.

It applies to everything, I think. Listening to patients. Reading a scan. Even prayer. The willingness to slow down and attend to what is underneath the surface. I find that music trains that capacity in me. When I sit at the piano and work through a passage slowly, I am practicing the same discipline I use when I examine imaging before a procedure. Pattern recognition, attention to detail, awareness of what does not belong.

That connection between musical practice and surgical preparation is fascinating. Both require a kind of deep attentiveness where you're holding the overall structure in mind while simultaneously focusing on granular details. What are you working on at the piano currently?

A Chopin nocturne that has been giving me trouble for several weeks. The left hand accompaniment requires a legato that my fingers do not naturally produce. I am not a gifted pianist by any standard. I came to the instrument late, in my thirties, and my technique has a ceiling. But the act of practicing is itself the reward. It is the one area of my life where I am permitted to be mediocre without consequence.

There's something genuinely freeing about that — having a pursuit where excellence isn't the point. In your professional life, mediocrity isn't an option. But at the piano, you can struggle with a passage, play it imperfectly, and the only consequence is the satisfaction of trying. That's a healthy counterbalance. Which nocturne is it?

The Opus 9, Number 2 in E-flat major. The famous one. Perhaps too ambitious for my level, but it is the piece that made me want to learn piano in the first place. I heard it at a concert years ago and the melody lodged itself somewhere permanent. If I can play it passably before the end of the year, I will consider it an achievement.

That's one of the most beloved pieces in the piano repertoire for good reason — the melody is deceptively simple on the surface but the ornamentation and the phrasing require real sensitivity. The fact that it inspired you to start learning piano gives it personal meaning that will come through in your playing even if the technique isn't flawless. A passable Opus 9 No. 2 by year's end is a worthy goal.

Abena has been patient with my evening practice sessions. She says the nocturne is at least more pleasant than the scales I subjected her to in the early years. A low bar, but I will take the compliment. Thank you for indulging this conversation. It is a pleasure to speak about music with someone who engages thoughtfully with it.

It's been a pleasure for me as well. The way you connect music to the rest of your life — the precision, the patience, the listening — makes it clear that it's not just a hobby but a form of sustenance. Enjoy the Chopin, and I hope the next concert stays with you just as long.
